title_en: The Spiritual Dictionary: Stations, States, and Their Definitions
content_en:
The heart of Ar-Risalah al-Qushayriyyah is the systematic dictionary of spiritual concepts that al-Qushayri compiled from the statements of the great early masters. This section of the work defines the key concepts of Islamic spiritual science with a precision that made the epistle the primary reference for subsequent scholars working in this tradition.

Al-Qushayri distinguishes carefully between maqamat (stations) and ahwal (states). Stations are spiritual levels that the believer acquires through sustained effort and that remain stable once attained. They are the result of the believer's own work — his repentance, his discipline, his striving against desire — though ultimately they are gifts from Allah in response to sincere effort. States, by contrast, are transient spiritual experiences that descend upon the heart from Allah without being fully under the believer's control. They come and go, sometimes elevating the believer to heights of joy, longing, or clarity, and then returning him to ordinary consciousness.

The major stations al-Qushayri defines include tawbah (repentance — turning from sin toward Allah, with genuine remorse and resolution), muhasabah (self-examination — the ongoing accounting of the heart's states and the soul's motivations), wara (scrupulousness — avoiding not only the forbidden but also the doubtful, and even the permissible when it risks drawing the heart away from Allah), zuhd (asceticism — detachment from worldly attractions through the heart's genuine preference for the hereafter), and tawakkul (complete reliance on Allah after taking legitimate means).

The major states al-Qushayri defines include muraqabah (watchfulness — the experiential sense of Allah's awareness of the believer's inner states), qurb (nearness — the spiritual experience of closeness to Allah through sincere worship and remembrance), mahabbah (love — the heart's natural orientation toward Allah when freed from worldly attachments), shawq (longing — the ache for nearness to Allah that motivates sustained spiritual effort), and uns (intimacy — the comfortable familiarity with Allah's presence that comes from long companionship in prayer and remembrance).

Each definition is supported by quotations from multiple early masters, allowing the reader to see how the same concept was understood across different times and personalities in the Sufi tradition. This comparative presentation is one of the features that made the epistle invaluable as a reference — it is simultaneously a dictionary, an anthology, and a history of how spiritual concepts developed in the Islamic tradition.
